title: @dataclass Basics

overview.what:
The `@dataclass` decorator in Python automatically generates boilerplate code for classes that primarily store data. When you add it to a class with type-hinted attributes, it automatically writes the `__init__()`, `__repr__()`, and `__eq__()` methods for you behind the scenes.

The mental model is a 'code generator': the decorator inspects the class at definition time, looks at the type hints, and injects the standard dunder methods into the class dictionary as if you had typed them yourself.
overview.why: It eliminates the repetitive and error-prone work of writing `def __init__(self, x): self.x = x` for every data object.

why_learning_this: You can define a class with 5 attributes in 6 lines of code instead of 20.
Your classes automatically get a readable string representation and value-based equality comparison.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting type hints
common_mistakes.explanation: The `@dataclass` decorator only recognizes fields that have type annotations. A plain assignment like `x = 5` in the class body will be completely ignored by the dataclass generator.

common_mistakes.title: Default mutable arguments
common_mistakes.explanation: If you assign a mutable default like `items: list = []`, all instances will share the same list. Dataclasses protect against this by raising a ValueError, but it catches beginners off guard.

recall_questions.q: Which three dunder methods does `@dataclass` automatically generate by default?
How does the dataclass decorator know which attributes to include in the generated methods?
recall_questions.answer: It looks at the class attributes that have type annotations.

understanding_checks.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: int
    y = 0

p = Point(5)
print(p)
understanding_checks.question: What does this code print?
understanding_checks.why: The attribute 'y' has no type hint, so @dataclass ignores it. It only generates an __init__ and __repr__ for the annotated 'x' field.

understanding_checks.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class User:
    name: str

u1 = User('Alice')
u2 = User('Alice')
print(u1 == u2)
understanding_checks.question: What does this code print?
understanding_checks.why: The @dataclass decorator automatically generates an __eq__ method that compares the instances' attributes. Since both have name='Alice', they evaluate as equal.

code_walkthrough.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Product:
    name: str
    price: float

item = Product('Apple', 1.5)
print(item)

aha_moment.prediction: Do two standard classes with identical attributes equal each other?
aha_moment.common_guess: True, because they have the same data.
aha_moment.why: It prints False. By default, custom classes use identity (memory address) for equality. Dataclasses fix this by automatically generating an __eq__ method for value equality.

practice_tasks.title: Convert to dataclass
practice_tasks.prompt: Convert this standard class into a dataclass to remove the boilerplate `__init__` and `__repr__` methods.

challenge.title: Dataclass with Defaults
challenge.prompt: Create a dataclass `Server` with a `host` (str) and a `port` (int). Set the default value of `port` to 80.
